Article

A 2 P.M. Meeting Costs Me Five Hours, Not One

The Monthly Rabbit Hole: Paul Graham figured out why a single meeting wrecks a whole day of creative work.

March 22, 2026 · 1 min read

#get-shit-done #tiny-reps #note-to-self

This month I finally understood why some days feel productive and some days just evaporate. Paul Graham wrote a blog post years ago called “Maker’s Schedule, Manager’s Schedule” and it explained something I’d been feeling but couldn’t articulate.

The manager schedule is what we all aspire toward. Back-to-back meetings from 9 to 6. You finish one, walk into the next. It looks productive because important people do it.

The maker schedule is different. You block your entire day for one deep task. Writing code, drafting a research paper, working through a hard proof. No meetings sprinkled in. No “quick catch-up” at 2 p.m. that destroys your entire afternoon.

I’ve noticed this firsthand with my writing. If I have a meeting at 2, my brain starts preparing for it at noon. I can’t focus after it ends until at least 3:30. By then, the day is gone. That single meeting didn’t cost one hour. It cost five.

Now I try to stack all my meetings on one day and protect the other days entirely. My own writing improved noticeably once I started doing this. Everything creative requires a maker schedule. And yes, solving equations counts as creative work.

If your real work needs long, unbroken stretches, guard them like they’re load-bearing. Because they are.
